coredata.dc:title: Active acoustic emission sensing for fast co-estimation of state of charge and state of health of the lithium-ion battery
coredata.prism:copyright: Crown Copyright © 2023 Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zuolu
Zhao, Xiaoyu
Zhang, Hao
Zhen, Dong
Gu, Fengshou
Ball, Andrew
coredata.dc:description: With the rapid development of clean energy technologies, the lithium-ion batteries have emerged as dominant power source. It is of great significance to monitor the state of charge (SOC) and state of health (SOH) accurately and efficiently for ensuing high safety and reliability. This paper proposes an active acoustic emission (AE) sensing technology and demonstrates the feasibility for co-estimation of SOC and SOH of the lithium-ion battery. The proposed method aims to achieve a fast monitoring of SOC and SOH by putting insights into the variation of material properties during operations. In the method, the appropriate power ultrasound is used to excite the battery and trigger elastic waves. A wide AE transducer can actively capture the released AE events of the battery material with different properties in a wide range of frequency band. Therefore, this allows the battery SOC/SOH to be fast characterised during charging, discharging, and ageing. Experimental studies have found that calculated RMS of AE signals in the frequency band 270–300 kHz, around the 7th harmonic of ultrasonic excitation, can successfully achieve the joint estimation of battery SOC and SOH at any operating phases. This new finding can be designed as a standalone method for fast SOC/SOH estimation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
State of charge
State of health
Material properties
Acoustic emission
Power ultrasound

The authors would like to express the appreciation to the Centre for Efficiency and Performance Engineering (CEPE) at University of Huddersfield for supporting the accomplishment of this research.

The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. 52175085 . The authors would like to express the appreciation to the Centre for Efficiency and Performance Engineering (CEPE) at University of Huddersfield for supporting the accomplishment of this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Multi-layered body structure of the pouch battery.
Schematic diagram of (a) power ultrasound and AE test rig, (b) layout of the pouch battery in the temperature chamber, and (c) the pouch lithium-ion battery.
(a) The terminal voltage profiles, (b) the variation of the battery surface temperature under different cycles, (c) the actual battery capacity with cycles and (d) SOH with cycles.

Time-domain AE signals measured at different stages. (a) Discharge stage with SOC = 100 %; (b) discharge stage with SOC = 65.24 %; (c) the first rest stage with SOC = 0 %; (d) the second stage with SOC = 0 %; (e) charge stage with SOC = 39.38 %; (f) charge stage with SOC = 85.43 %.

Spectrum of AE signals measured at different phases. (a) Discharge phase with SOC = 100 %; (b) discharge phase with SOC = 65.24 %; (c) the first rest phase with SOC = 0 %; (d) the second phase with SOC = 0 %; (e) charge phase with SOC = 39.38 %; (f) charge phase with SOC = 85.43 %.
AE RMS versus SOC in different frequency band: (a) 70–400 kHz, (b) 75–90 kHz, (c) 110–150 kHz, (d) 150–180 kHz, (e) 185–215 kHz, (f) 220–260 kHz, and (g) 270–300 kHz under different cycles.

The first-order polynomial fitting between the RMS and the battery SOC at (a) discharging process with 0 cycle, (b) charging process with 0 cycle, (c) discharging process with 20 cycles, (d) charging process with 20 cycles, (e) discharging process with 40 cycles, and (f) charging process with 40 cycles.
RMS variation calculated using the frequency band (a) 70–400 kHz, (b) 75–90 kHz, (c) 110–150 kHz, (d) 150–180 kHz, (e) 185–215 kHz, (f) 220–260 kHz, and (g) 270–300 kHz under different SOHs.

The first-order polynomial fitting relationship between SOH and RMS calculated using the frequency band 270–300 kHz at (a) before test phase when SOC = 100 %, (b) discharge phase when SOC = 90.3 %, (c) rest phase when SOC = 0 %, and phase stage when SOC = 100 %.
The correlation coefficients between the battery SOH and the calculated RMS using different frequency bands.

head.ce:title: Active acoustic emission sensing for fast co-estimation of state of charge and state of health of the lithium-ion battery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Efficiency and Performance Engineering, University of Huddersfield, Huddersfield HD1 3DH, UK

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Efficiency and Performance Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Efficiency and Performance Engineering, University of Huddersfield, Huddersfield, HD1 3DH, UK

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Power Transmission and Safety Technology for New Energy Vehicles, School of Mechanical Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300401, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Power Transmission and Safety Technology for New Energy Vehicles

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Power Transmission and Safety Technology for New Energy Vehicles, School of Mechanical Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin, 300401, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the rapid development of clean energy technologies, the lithium-ion batteries have emerged as dominant power source. It is of great significance to monitor the state of charge (SOC) and state of health (SOH) accurately and efficiently for ensuing high safety and reliability. This paper proposes an active acoustic emission (AE) sensing technology and demonstrates the feasibility for co-estimation of SOC and SOH of the lithium-ion battery. The proposed method aims to achieve a fast monitoring of SOC and SOH by putting insights into the variation of material properties during operations. In the method, the appropriate power ultrasound is used to excite the battery and trigger elastic waves. A wide AE transducer can actively capture the released AE events of the battery material with different properties in a wide range of frequency band. Therefore, this allows the battery SOC/SOH to be fast characterised during charging, discharging, and ageing. Experimental studies have found that calculated RMS of AE signals in the frequency band 270–300 kHz, around the 7th harmonic of ultrasonic excitation, can successfully achieve the joint estimation of battery SOC and SOH at any operating phases. This new finding can be designed as a standalone method for fast SOC/SOH estimation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: A variety of clean energy technologies, such as electric vehicles (EVs) and battery energy storage systems (BESSs), have been developing progressively for dealing with the energy crisis and experimental pollution [1,2]. The rapid expansion of these sustainable technologies is thanks to lithium-ion batteries that have emerged as a dominant power source with the high power density and long lifespan [3–5]. However, lithium-ion batteries are susceptible to thermal runway due to inappropriate operations (e.g., over-charging/discharging, and high rate) and external effects (e.g., high force and shock) [6,7]. The battery management system (BMS) plays an essential part in monitoring and controlling the battery states, in which the state of charge (SOC) and state of health (SOH) are two fundamental estimates.

The SOC presents the remaining capacity of the battery in the specific charging or discharging process, while the SOH delivers the ageing state of the battery in long-term cycles. They can be defined as follows [8]:
(1)
SOC =
C r
C a
× 100 %
(2)
SOH =
C a
C rated
× 100 %
where
C r
,
C a
, and
C rated
denote the remaining capacity, actual maximum capacity, and the rated capacity of the battery.

Accurate SOC evaluation can facilitate EV operations, such as battery equalization and optimal charge/discharge control. Meantime, precise SOH knowledge can contribute to SOC estimation and deliver an essential earning when batteries need to be replaced with new ones [9]. However, they are highly coupled, and their accurate estimations are easily subject to complex charge/discharge conditions and time-varying ambient temperatures. Therefore, developing accurate and efficient battery management technologies is still challenging work and a popular research topic.

A variety of methods have been developed for SOC/SOH estimation of lithium-ion batteries. Wang et al. [8] have classified the online estimation technologies of SOC/SOH by considering their applications in practice. It is worth noting that the model-based and data-driven methods show excellent performance on the online SOC/SOH estimation. For example, multiple popular filtering approaches, such as the adaptive extended Kalman filtering algorithm (AEKF) [10], improved unscented Kalman filter (IUKF) [11], adaptive fifth-degree cubature Kalman filter (AFDCKF) [12], central difference Kalman filter (CDKF) [13] and so on, were successfully applied to battery SOC estimation based on the equivalent electrical circuit model (EECM). In terms of the data-driven methods, the raw data (e.g., terminal voltage, current, and temperature) have been sufficiently considered as training features for modelling and SOC prediction [14–17]. Moreover, the data-driven methods are preferred choices for the battery SOH prediction with the help of the availability of the battery dataset. This is due to that the SOH is a long-term estimation and the data-driven methods do not need to understand the physical dynamics, which shows high flexibility and adaptability. Many researchers have dedicated to the extraction of the degradation feature from both the charging or discharging process for SOH prediction based on various machine learning methods, such as hybrid neural network-gate recurrent unit-convolutional neural network (GRU-CNN) [18], adaptive tuneable hybrid radial basis function network [19], variable-length-input long short-term memory (VLR-LSTM) network [20], extreme learning machine (ELM) [21] and hybrid kernel function relevance vector machine (HKRVM) [22]. Although these methods have proven effective for SOC/SOH estimation, they only focus on individual estimation. It is technologically challenging to conduct the co-estimation of SOC and SOH.

Many efforts have been made to develop the joint estimation strategies of these two essential states. For example, the EECM has been widely adopted for joint estimation as it can provide accurate SOC estimation by reproducing the dynamics of the battery and the model parameters also show a high correlation with the capacity degradation. Li et al. [23] used the adaptive extended H-infinity filter (AEHF) and particle swarm optimization (PSO) for the simultaneous SOC and SOH estimations based on the 2RC model. Similarly, the second-order equivalent circuit model was used to characterise the electrochemical characteristics of lithium-ion batteries in which the long short-term memory recurrent neural network (LSTM) was used for SOH prediction using the health feature of charging duration, and the square root cubature Kalman filter (SRCKF) was applied to SOC estimation based on the predicted capacity and updated model parameters [24]. To avoid complex model parameter identification and updating, Song et al. [25] used the data-driven least square support vector machine (LSSVM) method and unscented particle filter (UPF) to conduct the optimal SOC and SOH estimations based on the extracted feature from the terminal voltage and current. Nevertheless, these methods can indirectly achieve the battery SOC/SOH estimation as their applications highly depend on electrical measurements, such as voltage, loading current, and temperature. This undoubtedly increases the estimation complexity whether they are applied to online or offline scenarios. From the perspective of the electrochemical model (ECM), Liu et al. [26] proposed an original method for the joint estimation of SOC and SOH using the pseudo-two-dimensional (P2D) model. In the method, the lithium-ion concentration estimated particle filter (PF) algorithm was employed to evaluate the SOC and SOH. However, the partial differential equations in the ECM can introduce considerable computational costs.

Furthermore, a variety of advanced sensing-based techniques have been developed for the non-destructive monitoring of battery SOC/SOH. Based on the high-frequency AC excitation, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is used to monitor the battery's internal impedance that generally changes with charging/discharging and ageing [27–29]. However, the impedance spectrum is acquired through a wide range of frequency excitation, which increases the computational complexity. Moreover, monitoring ultrasound technology is focused on the variation of the battery material properties (e.g., elastic modulus and hardness) for simultaneous battery state estimations. Ladpli et al. [30] proposed ultrasonic-guided waves to monitor the battery states for the first time. It is validated that time of flight (TOF) and signal amplitude (SA) show a high correlation with battery SOC/SOH, which reveals that the battery material properties vary during the charge-discharge process and cycling. To be specific, the experimental studies indicate that the SA decreases as SOC decreases and increases as SOH decreases, whereas TOF changes inversely. According to SA and TOF of the echo pulses, many studies have been dedicated to SOC and SOH evaluations [31–34]. It is expected that the monitoring methods that put insights into the changes in battery material properties can achieve convenient, fast, and accurate co-estimation of SOC and SOH. However, the current ultrasound-based approaches are focused on the evaluation of the single frequency pulse. Therefore, only SA and TOF are considered as two essential characteristics for battery state estimations.

In consideration to the variation of battery material properties, this paper develops an active AE sensing technology for the fast joint estimation of SOC/SOH. The developed monitoring technology involves the applications of power ultrasound and acoustic emission (AE). Specifically, the appropriate power ultrasound is used to excite the battery and trigger AE events, while the AE transducer actively captures the elastic waves released by battery materials with different properties to characterise the battery state. The experimental studies show that the RMS is an effective feature to distinguish the battery SOC and SOH during the process of the charge-discharge cycle and ageing. It shows the RMS decreases with the decreasing of battery SOC and SOH. The frequency band 270–300 kHz with 7
f 0
can successfully achieve the simultaneous estimation of battery SOC and SOH at any SOC area.

The rest of the paper is organised as follows: Section 2 presents the theoretical background. Section 3 introduces details of the developed experimental rig and the test procedures. The results from the experimental studies are fully discussed in Section 4. Finally, conclusions are given in Section 5.

The lithium-ion battery is generally composed of anode, cathode, and electrolyte, in which the positive and negative electrodes play a crucial part in storing the lithium ions. The organic electrolyte between two electrodes is also essential for conducting lithium ions inside the battery [35,36]. Therefore, the pouch battery can be considered as a multi-layered body structure. The cathode and anode are coated on aluminium and copper foils, and they are separated by the separator as shown in Fig. 1 .

Different layered materials present different structural properties, which introduces a range of acoustic impedances in the battery. The acoustic impedance of the battery material can be defined as [37]:
(3)
Z = ρc
where ρ and c present the mass density of the material and the wave velocity, respectively. As a result, the reflection and transmission will occur when sound waves pass between two materials with different values of acoustic impedances. The reflection coefficient can be calculated as [32]:
(4)
R =
Z 2
−
Z 1
Z 2
+
Z 1
where
Z 1
and
Z 2
stand for the acoustic impedance of two different layers of battery materials, respectively. The reflection coefficient ranges from 0 and 1, hence 1 − R denotes the transmission coefficient.

The battery material properties can vary with charge-discharge cycles and ageing of the battery. In the short term, the insertion and disinsertion of lithium ions between electrodes during the process of charging and discharging can change the modulus and density of the battery material. It has been proposed that the damping increases, and the density and elastic modulus decreases with the decreasing of SOC [33]. In addition, long-term cycling can deplete the active material, which will lead to the formation of SEI and the degradation of electrolytes. Compared to the variation of the structural properties during charging or discharging, this can introduce irreversible changes in material properties. It shows that the elastic modulus and density of the electrode material decrease with the increase of the cycle number [38]. In summary, the battery material properties undergo regular changes during short-term operations. For example, elastic modulus and density show a trend of decreasing first and then rising during a discharging-charging process. However, the battery material properties generally show a decreasing trend with degradation. These short-term and long-term variations in various material properties can change the reflection coefficient R , which hence impacts the propagation of the acoustic wave and causes the different level of attenuations of the acoustic transmission in the battery. Therefore, the battery SOC and SOH can be seen as the function of the internal material property. Both the battery SOC and SOH can be estimated by monitoring changes in the internal material property.

It is considered that the battery material properties can be seen as the function of the SOC and SOH. The proposed method aims to perceive the variation of the material properties and then characterise battery states. It involves the applications of power ultrasound and AE technologies.

On the one hand, the appropriate power ultrasound is used to excite the battery and trigger AE events. The role of power ultrasound is mainly involved in mechanical and chemical effects [39]. The mechanical effects are primarily attributed to shear force and shock waves when transmitting the ultrasonic wave to the solid or liquid medium. The mechanical process can accelerate the movement of ions and thus accelerate electrochemical reactions [37]. Based on the mechanical and chemical effects, the appropriate power ultrasound on the battery can induce micro deformations and excite more AE events that probably change with the change of the battery material properties.

On the other hand, AE transducers actively capture the elastic waves released by battery materials with different properties to characterise the battery states. AE technique has developed into an effective monitoring method by capturing the quick elastic waves or transient waves arising from the localised material; however, it is difficult for conventional sound and vibration sensors to collect high-frequency information. When the power ultrasound is propagated in the battery, the layered structure of the battery causes attenuation, and the ultrasound can cause stress waves in the electrodes. The propagated ultrasound and the generated stress wave signals can be collected by the AE sensor and then analysed for sensing changes in battery material properties. During the charging-discharging operation or cycling, the battery material properties will be changed, thus resulting in different acoustic impedances and AE events. Furthermore, the AE transducer has the advantage of achieving the conversion of the narrowband signal to the wideband signal. Therefore, the AE method can ensure sufficient sensing of changes in the battery material property, thus achieving more accurate SOC and SOH estimation.

Based on the fundamental excitation frequency
f 0
, a wide range of frequency components can be obtained, and they are described as follows [40,41]:
•
The fundamental frequency
f 0
: It is produced by the excitation frequency.
•
Harmonics of the fundamental frequency
n
f 0
(where n denotes a positive integer): They are caused by the transducer itself and probably the nonlinear propagation in the battery.
•
Subharmonics of the fundamental
f 0
/ n : They are excited by bubbles that are twice the resonance size or from non-spherical bubbles with surface oscillations.
•
Ultra-harmonics of the fundamental
m
f 0
/ n
(where m is a positive integer, m > n , and m / n is not integral): They are produced by the bubble events that are the same as the source of subharmonics of the fundamental.
•
Broadband signal: They are seen as noises in all frequencies, and they are related to transient cavitation.

During the short-term charge and discharge process, the collected AE signal can be used for SOC estimation. In long-term cycles, the AE signal measured under different level of degradations can be analysed and compared for the SOH estimation. Based on the above theoretical analysis, it aims to explore and evaluate the performance of the proposed the active AE sensing method for SOC/SOH monitoring.

To carry out the performance analysis of the proposed active AE sensing technology for the fast monitoring of the battery states, a test system is developed based on a narrow-band power ultrasound transducer and a wide-band AE sensor. Fig. 2 (a) illustrates the schematic diagram of the designed test rig. It is composed of host computer, battery tester, temperature chamber, data acquisition system, AE acquisition system, preamplifier, AE sensor, power ultrasound generator, ultrasonic vibrator, DC power supply, current clamp, pouch battery, thermocouple, and G clamp.

In the test, an EBC-A20 battery tester is used to charge and discharge the battery. The 16-bit-data acquisition system with a sampling frequency of 10 Hz is responsible for the collection of voltage, current, and temperature. Fig. 2 (b) presents the layout of the pouch battery inside the temperature chamber. The temperature chamber can warm up to a maximum of 65 °C and cool down to a max of 25 °C below the ambient temperature with ±0.3 °C accuracy. Particularly, the test temperature is set as 25 °C to avoid the influence of the time-varying ambient temperature. On one side of the battery, the surface-mounted thermocouple probe is used to monitor the battery surface's temperature during operations. The amplifier integrated cold-junction compensation is adopted to guarantee precise temperature measurement. It is supplied by the DC power supply with a stable output of 10 V and its measurement sensitivity is 100 mv/°C. The AE sensor is used to collect the AE signal at the sampling frequency of 2 Mhz. The 40 dB preamplifier can amplify the weak voltage signal output from the AE sensor. On the opposite side of the battery, the ultrasonic vibrator with a resonance frequency of 28 kHz is used to transmit the power ultrasound that is generated by the generator with the excitation frequency of 40 kHz. An adjustable G clamp is utilized to fix all the devices during the test. In this test, a low-power ultrasonic square wave is used to excite the battery and the actual power can be evaluated by the amplitude of the acquired AE signal. The targeted pouch battery is displayed in Fig. 2 (c), and the key specifications are listed in Table 1 .

To check the effectiveness of the proposed rapid SOC/SOH estimation method, the pouch battery tests are designed based on the established power ultrasound and AE test rig. The battery tests involve three processes: charge, discharge, and cycle. The detailed test schemes are given as follows:
•
Charge: The battery is charged using CC-CV mode until the current drops to 0.1C. The charging current and voltage are selected as 0.5C-rate and 4.2 V, respectively. Then the battery is kept at rest for one hour to reach a steady state before entering the discharge test.
•
Discharge: The battery is discharged using CC mode until the terminal voltage decreases to the discharge cut-off voltage of 3 V. The discharging current is set at 0.5C-rate.
•
Cycle: The cycle tests aim to degrade the battery. One cycle test includes a complete charging and discharging process. The charge/discharge current and voltage are the same as the above settings. Specifically, the battery will have a rest for 10 min before going into the charging or discharging process. After 20 cycles, the battery SOH is recalibrated based on the available remaining battery capacity.

During the test, the power ultrasound is operated on four parts: before discharge, discharge, rest, and charge, and the measured AE signals under different time points are utilized to characterise the battery SOC. The characteristic parameters obtained under different cycles can be used to distinguish different battery SOHs. In terms of AE signal measurements, the power ultrasound was first input and lasted for 10 s, and then the AE signal was collected at the 5th second and the collection lasted for 5 s. In the processes of discharging and charging, the power ultrasound excitation and AE signal collection were carried out per 10 min. Especially, AE signals were also measured in the rest phase. After discharging, the resting stage lasted for 20 min, and the AE signal was collected at an interval of 5 min, while the first 5 min was only for relaxing without ultrasonic excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time and frequency domain analysis for feature selection
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the experimental results are discussed in terms of the validation for joint SOC and SOH estimations. Fig. 3 displays the terminal voltage profiles, variation of the battery surface temperature, capacity, and SOH under different cycles. The terminal voltage collection involves three stages, including discharge, rest, and charge, as shown in Fig. 3 (a). In general, the battery discharging can release heat and hence cause the rise of the temperature while the battery temperature drops in the charging process. As illustrated in Fig. 3 (b), the maximum fluctuation of battery surface temperature does not exceed three degrees in the process of charging and discharging under different cycles. It indicates that the external power ultrasound excitation does not bring overly drastic changes to the battery. It is validated that the appropriate power ultrasound input does not damage the battery and affect normal operations.

Fig. 3 (c) shows the degradation of the battery capacity with cycles. The released capacity is 1920 mAh at the initial stage where the battery SOH is regarded as 100 %. The battery SOH can be calculated under different levels of degradation. As can be seen from Fig. 3 (d), the battery life degrades to about 96.5 % after 120 cycles.

Taking the battery tests at 100 % SOH as an example, Fig. 4 illustrates the time-domain AE signals measured in different working conditions, including discharge, rest, and charge parts. The AE signal amplitudes show differences at different stages of SOCs. Fig. 4 (a) and Fig. 4 (b) show the AE waveforms at discharging stages when SOC is 100 % and 65.24 %, respectively. It can be seen the AE signal amplitudes when SOC is 65.24 % are a little bit larger than that of 100 % SOC. As for two rest stages, as displayed in Fig. 4 (c) and Fig. 4 (d), the AE signal amplitudes appear to be nearly the same at 0 % SOC. Fig. 4 (e) and Fig. 4 (f) illustrate the collected AE signals at charging stages when SOC is 39.38 % and 85.43 %. Obviously, the AE signal amplitudes at 39.38 % SOC are smaller than those measured at 85.43 % SOC. The battery material properties change with the charging or discharging, resulting in different amplitudes in the measured AE signals.

Furthermore, Fig. 5 shows the spectrum of the original signals as shown in Fig. 4. All the spectra are presented from 0 kHz to 500 kHz due to the selected digital filter in the AE acquisition system. Basically, the frequency components are distributed between 70 kHz and 300 kHz. The measured AE signals under different states present the same frequency components in the spectrum. As shown in Fig. 5, the frequency components are mainly the harmonics of the fundamental excitation frequency
f 0
induced by the power ultrasound, including 2
f 0
, 3
f 0
, 4
f 0
, 5
f 0
, 6
f 0
, and 7
f 0
. Around these main frequency components, we can get a series of frequency bands. It is assumed that different frequency bands exhibit different responses to the time-varying states of the battery. Therefore, it is necessary to explore the performance of using various frequency bands for battery SOC/SOH monitoring.

According to the above analysis, the root mean square (RMS) is adopted to analyse the AE signals in different frequency bands for the characterization of the battery SOC/SOH. RMS is a statistical feature that can reflect the error dispersion of experimental results. It is defined as the square root of the mean square, and it is expressed as:
(5)
RMS =
∑
i = 1
N
X i 2
N
where
X i
denotes the ith sample and N stands for the number of samples.

Based on the frequency-domain analysis, the collected AE signal is finally divided into several frequency bands, including 70–400 kHz, 75–90 kHz, 110–150 kHz, 150–180 kHz, 185–215 kHz, 220–260 kHz, and 270–300 kHz. In particular, 70–400 kHz is used to represent the whole frequency band, and other frequency bands are chosen to include the prominent harmonics of the fundamental excitation frequency
f 0
.

Fig. 6 shows the changing trend of the calculated RMS with SOC under different cycles. Particularly, the determined frequency bands are filtered to present the responses of the characteristic parameter RMS to the battery SOC. For example, Fig. 6 (a) shows the RMS change chart with SOC using the filtered frequency band 70–400 kHz, in which the AE signal collection and RMS calculation are included in four parts, and lines with different colours represent the results under different cycles ranging from 0 to 120. It can be clearly seen that the calculated RMS using various frequency bands present different relationships with the battery SOC. As shown in Fig. 6 (b) and (e), the calculated RMS in both frequency bands 75–90 kHz and 185–215 kHz fail to characterise the battery SOC. There is no clear changing trend that can be found with SOC. For instance, the calculated RMS of AE signals after 40 cycles show nearly constant under different SOCs. Similarly, the frequency band 185–215 kHz also cannot be effectively used for battery SOC estimation. It can be seen from Fig. 6 (e) that the calculated RMS decreases between 100 % SOC and 60 % SOC during the discharging phase and increases from 60 % SOC to 100 % SOC in the charging stage. The low SOC areas cannot be identified based on the RMS calculation in this frequency band. However, the other frequency bands, including 70–400 kHz, 110–150 kHz, 150–180 kHz, 220–260 kHz, and 270–300 kHz, can recognise the different SOCs based on the RMS calculation to some extent. It shows that the calculated RMS decreases over SOC in the discharging stage, keeps constant in the resting stage, and gradually increases in the charging stage. In contrast, the RMS of the AE signal in the frequency band 270–300 kHz with 7
f 0
can offer more apparent SOC distinction under different cycles.

Therefore, the frequency band 270–300 kHz is chosen to further present the relationship between the calculated RMS and battery SOC. For example, Fig. 7 shows the first-order polynomial fitting relationship between the calculated RMS and the battery SOC from 0 cycle to 40 cycles. In particular, both R- Squared and RMSE are used to quantify the linear fitting between the actual and fitting RMS. It is noted that the high R- Squared and low RMSE present a high linear correlation between RMS and the battery SOC.

As mentioned previously, the material properties of the battery change as lithium ions move between two electrodes. In particular, the damping of the battery material increases with power consumption in the discharging stage, and the modulus exhibits the opposite change to the damping. This can lead to more considerable attenuation of elastic waves measured by the AE sensor with the decrease of SOC. Therefore, it appears that the calculated RMS of the AE signal is small when the SOC is low. According to the experimental results, it can be found that the calculated RMS is quite sensitive to the variation of the battery material properties so it is proven effective for the battery SOC estimation.

To investigate the performance of the proposed monitoring technology for SOH estimation, 120 cycles are carried out to degrade the battery as shown in Fig. 3 (c) and Fig. 3 (d). Fig. 8 illustrates the variation of RMS calculated using different frequency bands with SOHs. Obviously, the calculated RMS also exhibit different responses to battery SOH. For example, the RMS values calculated in the frequency bands, such as 70–400 kHz, 110–150 kHz, and 150–180 kHz, present higher sensitivity to the battery SOH. It is mentioned that the RMS values decrease with the degradation of the battery. However, the calculated RMS values in the frequency band of 185–215 kHz fluctuate randomly, which does not show an obvious change rule and cannot effectively characterise the variation of the battery SOH. Furthermore, the RMS calculated at different SOC areas have different mapping relationships with battery SOH. In terms of the frequency bands 70–400 kHz, 110–150 kHz and 220–260 kHz, the RMS values can better distinguish the battery SOH at low SOC areas, such as between 0 % SOC and 50 % SOC. However, the RMS values show a decreasing trend with SOH at higher SOC regions in the frequency bands of 150–180 kHz and 185–215 kHz. By contrast, the RMS calculated in 270–300 kHz provide a better corresponding relationship with battery SOH at any SOCs, as shown in Fig. 8 (g).

All in all, it is noted that the calculated characteristic parameter RMS can also distinguish different SOHs. It shows a downward trend with the decreasing of battery SOH. It indicates that the collected AE signal gradually attenuates in the process of the battery ageing. As mentioned above, the degradation of the battery can consume lithium ions and reduce the elastic modulus and hardness of the electrode material, which hence increases the attenuation of sound waves.

Table 2 compares the calculated correlation coefficients between the battery SOH and the obtained RMS values at a wide range of SOC regions using different frequency bands. Specifically, the AE elastic waves acquired in the first 5 min of the resting stage are used to calculate the correlation between battery SOH and RMS values. Overall, the frequency bands, including 70–400 kHz, 75–90 kHz, 110–150 kHz, and 270–300 kHz, can provide a high correlation between SOH and the characteristic parameter RMS. It is noticed that the high-frequency band 270–300 kHz with 7
f 0
can achieve SOH recognition at any SOC region and help provide a higher correlation with the battery SOH.

In particular, Fig. 9 illustrates the first-order polynomial fitting relationship between SOH and RMS calculated using the frequency band 270–300 kHz at four different stages. According to the R-Squared and RMSE, it shows that the calculated RMS values are strongly correlated with the battery SOH. More importantly, this frequency band can indicate the changing rule that the RMS value decreases as SOC decreases and increases as SOC increases.

This paper proposes a novel active AE sensing technology for the first time to perform the fast co-estimation of battery SOC and SOH. This method was developed based on the variation of battery material properties during the charge-discharge cycle, in which the power ultrasound can excite AE events and the AE transducer can actively perceive more battery state information in a broad frequency band. Through the time-domain analysis, it is found that the collected AE signal energy presents differences at different SOCs or SOHs. Besides, the frequency-domain analysis reveals that a series of harmonics appear in the spectrum, such as 2
f 0
, 3
f 0
, 4
f 0
, 5
f 0
, 6
f 0
, and 7
f 0
. The statistical parameter RMS is selected as an essential feature to characterise the battery SOC/SOH using different frequency bands. The experimental studies reveal that the different frequency bands exhibit different sensitivities to the battery SOC/SOH. The bandpass RMS decreases with the decrease of battery SOC and SOH. The frequency band 270–300 kHz with 7
f 0
can successfully achieve the simultaneous estimation of battery SOC and SOH at any SOC area, verifying the feasibility and performance of this novel method.

Zuolu Wang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xiaoyu Zhao: Software, Validation. Hao Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Dong Zhen: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Fengshou Gu: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Supervision. Andrew Ball: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. 52175085. The authors would like to express the appreciation to the Centre for Efficiency and Performance Engineering (CEPE) at University of Huddersfield for supporting the accomplishment of this research.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An execution time optimized state of charge estimation method for lithium-ion battery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [1] B. Çelikten, O. Eren, and Y. S. Karataş, ‘An execution time optimized state of charge estimation method for lithium-ion battery’, Journal of Energy Storage, vol. 51, p. 104307, 2022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Constant current charging time based fast state-of-health estimation for lithium-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [2] C. Lin, J. Xu, M. Shi, and X. Mei, ‘Constant current charging time based fast state-of-health estimation for lithium-ion batteries’, Energy, vol. 247, p. 123556, 2022.
